question: Define a 'class' in the context of class-based modeling and identify its two main components.
expectedPoints: A class is a template or blueprint from which objects are created.
It describes a set of objects that share the same attributes, operations, relationships, and semantics.
The two main components are: 1) Attributes (or properties) which define the data/state of an object.
2) Operations (or methods) which define the behavior or functionality of an object.

question: Explain the purpose and primary steps involved in creating a Class-Responsibility-Collaborator (CRC) model.
expectedPoints: Purpose: To identify and organize the classes relevant to a software system in a simple, interactive way.
It helps in understanding the high-level relationships and responsibilities of classes early in the design process.
Primary Steps: 1) Identify classes (often from a noun analysis or domain analysis).
2) For each class, define its Responsibilities (what it knows or does).
3) Identify its Collaborators (other classes it interacts with to fulfill its responsibilities).
4) Organize the classes on index cards and have team members role-play the interactions to refine the model.

question: Compare and contrast the Class-Responsibility-Collaborator (CRC) model with a formal Class Diagram in UML. Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of each and explain in which phases of the design process they are most effectively used.
expectedPoints: CRC Model: Informal, text-based, focuses on behavior and collaboration. Best used in early analysis phases for brainstorming and initial class discovery. Advantage: Encourages team interaction and is simple to create and modify. Disadvantage: Lacks precision and formality for detailed design and implementation.

Class Diagram: Formal, graphical, detailed. It is the standard UML diagram for depicting static structure. Shows attributes, operations, visibility, relationships (associations, generalizations, dependencies) in detail. Advantage: Precise, unambiguous, and can be used to generate code skeletons. Disadvantage: Can be complex and time-consuming to create; less effective for initial brainstorming.

Phase Usage: CRC models are most effective in the early analysis and high-level design phase for exploring domain concepts. Class diagrams are used in the detailed design phase to specify the system's structure for implementation.
